text:
I grew up in a suburb, and when my musical tastes got more adventurous, I was kind of on my own culturally. I didn’t know anyone who cared about the music that I was interested in, which at that time meant trying to find ways to access physical CDs or records on my own, in a world before streaming or even before I could easily access filesharing.

I remember having to track down obscure albums by going to multiple record shops and hoping they had a copy. I’d go in with a list once a week to see if something cool and new had been delivered. Sometimes you’d have to mail order directly from the label (or artist!) just to have any chance to ever hear something. 

I mean that literally — there would literally be no way to access the piece of music in question unless you could track down a physical copy. If you knew the right time of day, you could sometimes call a college radio station and make a request, but you’d have to pick the right show or host for your taste. 

Finding something that you’d been  searching for for ages was such a triumph! 
It was a separate form of love from actually enjoying the music. 

Sometimes this form of collectors’ love overtook the joy of the music itself, but most of the time it served to enhance it. The experience was made more precious due to both effort and scarcity. 

I’d read about a song or artist or album for literally years, unable to track down a copy, having to imagine what it sounded like. 
I’d have an imagined version of the song or album that I could listen to with my
mind’s ear whenever I dreamed about finally hearing it. 

You will likely never know what it’s like to hear something for the first time, having read or heard about it for years without knowing what it was really like, imagining it when you weren’t sure if you’d ever get to experience it, wishing for some new employee to order a copy for sale on a whim, hoping that your check made it to some PO Box in Texas and that it would eventually be processed by anonymous label employees or the artist himself, waiting, waiting for something to arrive, and finally pressing play…

text:
I did a sauna + cold plunge recently, but the guide for the class covered up the different temperatures in the cold plunge pools. 
I didn’t know in advance but the plunge session was supposed to be 2 minutes.

So I hopped in the 1 degree C water not knowing what to expect and instantly couldn’t breathe. Limbs in pain. My brain turned off and I couldn’t move. I got out when the burning sharp numbness in my legs felt impossible to sustain. 20 seconds? 30? I could barely get out of the pool because my legs didn’t work and I couldn’t feel my feet properly.

In the next session, I was guided to a plunge pool that was a few degrees warmer (though I didn’t know this), and the physical sensation was not as overwhelming. However, the mental move of total surrender to the cold felt further away. I was fighting against it more, because I still had some of my mental faculties. I tried to move into a position where the pain would be less, but of course that’s not how you overcome it. 45 seconds or so but somehow this was harder than the first time, although the pain was less

text:
Post-Industrial Revolution economic development depends on new social & economic structures that we can call “modernity”

Modernity requires that society be organised like a factory, 

so humans get treated like factory farm animals, 

so they have their agency ground down by school and work and even social life, 

so they don’t learn emotionally maturity, 

so their emotional and physical maturity don’t match up, 

so their bodies are ready for children but their minds are not

so the birth rate declines 

so modernity and economic progress accelerate 

so we extend the systems and processes that stunt emotional growth

so emotional immaturity extends deeper into adulthood 

so the birth rate declines faster 

[we are here]

text:
A group of children sits on the floor looking up at their grandparents sitting on a sofa. 

The old couple is telling a story that they’ve told hundreds of times — to their friends, to their families, to their wedding guests, to their children and now to their grandchildren. It’s been repeated almost weekly for the last fifty years, and even though it’s well rehearsed it still feels vibrant and fresh because both of them relive every moment in the hearts every time they tell it. The grandchildren love hearing the story again even though they know what’s coming; they ask for it every time they come to visit. Maybe someday they’ll stop asking to hear it again, but for now it’s their deepest way to connect to the strangeness of the world their grandparents lived in, before the Great Acceleration. Even in its foreignness, the story tells the children something about themselves, about their history and about their hopes and dreams, too.

For most of the story he is the one speaking, while she mainly smiles and listens. Her silence is the perfect setup to the joke that’s also the punchline for their entire relationship. With perfect timing the old woman interrupts, as she’s done hundreds of times before, slightly cutting him off for comic effect, and says:

“of course, I did speak English, but I though he was much cuter trying to speak Chinese to me”

And here the story pauses for him to give her a kiss.

The old couple is telling a story that they’ve told hundreds of times — to their friends, to their families, to their wedding guests, to their children and now to their grandchildren. It’s been repeated almost weekly for the last fifty years, and even though it’s well rehearsed it still feels vibrant and fresh because both of them relive every moment in the hearts every time they tell it. The grandchildren love hearing the story again even though they know what’s coming; they ask for it every time they come to visit. Maybe someday they’ll stop asking to hear it again, but for now it’s their deepest way to connect to the strangeness of the world their grandparents lived in, before the Great Acceleration. Even in its foreignness, the story tells the children something about themselves, about their history and about their hopes and dreams, too.

For most of the story he is the one speaking, while she mainly smiles and listens. Her silence is the perfect setup to the joke that’s also the punchline for their entire relationship. With perfect timing the old woman interrupts, as she’s done hundreds of times before, slightly cutting him off for comic effect, and says:

“of course, I did speak English, but I though he was much cuter trying to speak Chinese to me”

And here the story pauses for him to give her a kiss.
